metadata.regulatory_authority: Health Canada — Health Products and Food Branch (HPFB)

governing_legislation.primary_act: Food and Drugs Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. F-27)

DIN.description: Unique 8-digit number assigned by Health Canada to each drug product authorized for sale in Canada
DIN.regulatory_basis: C.01.014 of Food and Drug Regulations

NOC.description: Issued when a drug submission meets regulatory requirements — equivalent to market authorization
NOC.regulatory_basis: C.08.004 of Food and Drug Regulations

NOC_c.description: Conditional approval mechanism for serious/life-threatening/severely debilitating diseases where evidence shows promising benefit; requires post-market confirmatory studies

drug_schedules.Schedule_D: Biologics (vaccines, blood products, sera, toxoids) — require lot release
drug_schedules.Schedule_F: Prescription drugs (now superseded by Prescription Drug List)
drug_schedules.Schedule_G: Controlled substances and advanced therapeutic products

NDS.regulatory_basis: C.08.002 of Food and Drug Regulations
NDS.description: Full submission for a new active substance or novel drug product not previously authorized in Canada
NDS.brand_name_confusion_assessment: C.08.002(2)(o) — NDS must include assessment of likelihood of brand name being mistaken for another drug — UNIQUE TO CANADA
NDS.population_subgroup_harmonization: C.08.002(2.01) — If clinical trial data is broken down by population subgroup in an EMA or FDA application, the same breakdown must appear in the Canadian NDS
canada_specific_requirements: Product Monograph in prescribed format (bilingual English/French)
Letters of Access (if referencing third-party data)

ANDS.regulatory_basis: C.08.002.1 of Food and Drug Regulations
ANDS.description: Submission for a subsequent-entry (generic) drug product that is pharmaceutical equivalent and bioequivalent to a Canadian Reference Product
eligibility_criteria: New drug is pharmaceutical equivalent of the Canadian Reference Product
New drug is bioequivalent to the CRP (based on pharmaceutical and bioavailability characteristics)
Same route of administration as the CRP
Conditions of use fall within the CRP's conditions of use
ANDS.fda_equivalent: ANDA (Abbreviated New Drug Application) under 21 CFR 314.94
canada_specific_requirements: Canadian Reference Product confirmation (purchase receipts showing trade name, DIN, lot #, expiry)
Comprehensive Summary: Bioequivalence (CS-BE) — Canada-unique summary document
Declaration of Equivalence (issued with NOC per C.08.004(4))
Comparative Bioavailability Data Summary Tables (Appendix A format)
BE data sets in computer-readable format (*.inf and *.dat ASCII files)

SNDS.regulatory_basis: C.08.003 of Food and Drug Regulations
SNDS.description: Submission for changes to an authorized NDS product (new indication, new dosage form, new route of administration, etc.)

SANDS.description: Supplement to an existing ANDS for changes to an authorized generic product

NC.description: Minor changes that require notification but not a supplement

CTA.regulatory_basis: Part C, Division 5 (C.05.001-C.05.016) of Food and Drug Regulations
CTA.description: Application filed with Health Canada prior to initiating a clinical trial in Canada
CTA.review_mechanism: Default approval — if no objection within 30 days, trial may proceed (No Objection Letter issued)

format.submission_format: Electronic media with hard copy cover letter, organized per non-eCTD electronic specifications

required_forms: HC/SC 3011 Drug Submission Application Form (signed by Senior Medical/Scientific Officer and Senior Executive Officer)
Protocol Safety and Efficacy Assessment Template (PSEAT-CTA)
Quality Overall Summary (QOS-CE for chemical entities)
scope: Phase I-III trials with drugs not authorized for sale in Canada
Trials with marketed drugs used outside NOC/DIN parameters
NOT required for Phase IV trials within approved NOC/DIN parameters
key_differences_from_fda_ind: CTA uses 30-day default approval (NOL); FDA IND also has 30-day review but uses clinical hold mechanism differently
CTA requires PSEAT-CTA template — no FDA equivalent template
CTA uses CTD modular format (Modules 1-3); IND uses different format structure
Canada requires separate CTA for each protocol; FDA allows multiple protocols under one IND
Research Ethics Board (REB) approval required at each site BEFORE trial commencement — explicitly codified in C.05.006(1)(c)
Qualified Investigator must be a physician licensed in the province where trial is conducted
Senior Medical/Scientific Officer must reside in Canada
key_differences_from_ema_cta: No centralized EU-CTR-style portal — submissions go to appropriate Directorate
REB (not Ethics Committee) requirements with specific composition rules (min 5 members, majority Canadian citizens)
Lot release information required for biologics before trial commencement
CTA-N (Notification) pathway for quality-only amendments — no EMA equivalent

CTA_A.regulatory_basis: C.05.008 of Food and Drug Regulations
types: CTA-A Clinical — for clinical protocol amendments
CTA-A Quality (CTA-N) — notification for chemistry and manufacturing changes

pre_CTA_consultation.description: Health Canada offers pre-CTA consultation meetings, particularly for new active substances or complex applications
requirements: Information package submitted 30 days before meeting
Written record of meeting sent to HC within 14 days

composition.gender: Composed of both men and women
required_expertise: Two members with scientific discipline expertise (one medical)
One member knowledgeable in Canadian laws relevant to the research
One community member not affiliated with sponsor or site

REB_requirements.key_difference: Canadian REB composition explicitly requires Canadian citizenship majority and specific knowledge areas codified in regulations

ADR_reporting.description: Adverse Drug Reaction reporting during clinical trials
ADR_reporting.reporting_destination: Canada Vigilance Production stream or appropriate Directorate (TPD, BGTD, or MHPD)
ADR_reporting.annual_summary_report: Required — Annual Summary Report and Case Reports per C.01.018

canadian_reference_product.definition: C.08.001.1 of Food and Drug Regulations

three_categories.description: A drug for which a NOC has been issued under C.08.004 and which is marketed in Canada by the innovator
A drug acceptable to the Minister for demonstrating bioequivalence, where the innovator's product is no longer marketed in Canada
A drug acceptable to the Minister for demonstrating bioequivalence in comparison to a category (a) product

canadian_reference_product.foreign_CRP_policy: A Canadian Reference Product purchased outside of Canada may be acceptable under category (c), but requires justification statement and supporting data (e.g., comparative dissolution)
confirmation_evidence: Signed confirmation that reference was purchased in Canada
Photocopy of product label(s) showing trade name, strength, lot #, expiry date, and DIN
canadian_reference_product.fda_equivalent: Reference Listed Drug (RLD) — listed in Orange Book
canadian_reference_product.key_divergence: Canada requires the reference product to be specifically marketed IN Canada by the innovator. FDA's RLD is any US-approved product. EMA allows reference product from any EU member state. Canada's foreign CRP pathway is more restrictive.

product_monograph.description: Canada-specific prescribing information document that serves as the comprehensive drug labelling
product_monograph.regulatory_basis: Required under NDS/ANDS submissions; format prescribed by Health Canada guidance
product_monograph.bilingual_requirement: Must be submitted in both English and French (Official Languages Act requirement)
product_monograph.format: XML-based template with prescribed structure (master-template.docx provided by HC)
product_monograph.submission_format: Validated XML format with specific validation rules
product_monograph.master_template: Single Master Template replaced 6 separate templates in 2020
product_monograph.legal_role: PM is the standard against which all promotion and advertising is measured. C.08.002(2) prohibits advertising a new drug for any use or claim not in the authorized PM.
title_page_requirements: Words 'Product Monograph, Including Patient Medication Information'
Scheduling symbol (Pr, N, T/C) preceding brand name
Therapeutic classification per WHO ATC index
Manufacturer name, place of business, website

numbered_sections.3: Serious Warnings and Precautions Box (may be omitted if none)
numbered_sections.4: Dosage and Administration (4.1-4.8 incl. Missed dose, Image acquisition, Radiopharmaceuticals, Radiation dosimetry)
numbered_sections.6: Dosage Forms, Strengths, Composition, and Packaging (6.1 Physical characteristics, 6.2 External radiation)
numbered_sections.7: Warnings and Precautions (7.1 Special populations: Pregnancy, Breastfeeding, Pediatrics, Geriatrics)
numbered_sections.9: Drug Interactions (9.1-9.7 incl. drug-behaviour, drug-herb, drug-lab test)
numbered_sections.12: Special Handling Instructions (may be omitted if none)

numbered_sections.14: Clinical Trials (14.1 By indication, 14.2 Comparative bioavailability)
numbered_sections.15: Microbiology (may be omitted if not applicable)
numbered_sections.17: Supporting Product Monographs (may be omitted if none)

three_parts.part: Patient Medication Information (PMI, formerly Part III: Consumer Information)
requirements: Plain language at Grade 6-8 reading level (Flesch-Kincaid/SMOG)
Separate style guide from Parts 1 and 2
Required for ALL drugs regardless of dispensing location
Must be available electronically on Drug Product Database and/or manufacturer website

product_monograph.section_numbering_rule: Section numbers are FIXED across all PMs and must not be renumbered or omitted (with 4 exceptions noted above)

style_requirements.no_promotional_words: Prohibited: unique, novel, convenient, potent, etc.

xml_format.controlled_vocabularies: Standardized terminology via Health Canada CVs
key_rules: DIN required as 8-digit number (may have leading zeros)
Non-Canadian manufacturer must have Canadian distributor/importer contactParty
Canadian postal codes in A1A 1A1 format
Images must be JPEG with .jpeg extension

bilingual_filing_timelines.second_language_deadline: No later than 20 days following NOC issuance

serious_warnings_box.description: Mandatory highlighted box at the beginning of the PM for serious warnings — unique Canadian format requirement
serious_warnings_box.ema_equivalent: No direct equivalent; warnings in SmPC Section 4.4
serious_warnings_box.key_difference: Canadian format integrates it as part of a structured 3-part document, not a standalone box on PI

comparative_bioavailability_table.description: Required for ANDS submissions — Summary Table of Comparative Bioavailability Data must be included in the PM
comparative_bioavailability_table.format: Prescribed format in Appendix A of CTD Bioavailability Guidance
comparative_bioavailability_table.fda_equivalent: Not required in PI for generics
comparative_bioavailability_table.ema_equivalent: Not required in SmPC for generics

key_differences_from_fda_pi: PM has 3 parts (HCP info, scientific info, patient info); FDA PI has 17 numbered sections per PLR format
PM requires bilingual (English/French); FDA PI is English only
PM Part III (Patient Medication Info) is integrated into the PM; FDA separates Patient Package Insert / Medication Guide
PM uses XML template with validation rules; FDA uses Structured Product Labeling (SPL) in XML
PM includes Comparative Bioavailability Tables for generics; FDA PI does not
PM includes Detailed Pharmacology and Toxicology in Part II; FDA PI does not include raw toxicology
PM Serious Warnings Box is structurally different from FDA Boxed Warning
key_differences_from_ema_smpc: PM has 3 parts; SmPC has 10 numbered sections under different format
PM includes Patient Medication Information as Part III; EMA separates Package Leaflet (PL) from SmPC
PM requires both English and French; SmPC is in the language of the member state
PM uses Health Canada's XML template; SmPC uses QRD template
PM includes full Clinical Trials data and Detailed Pharmacology; SmPC Section 5 is more concise
PM is a single document serving HCPs, scientists, and patients; EMA splits into SmPC + PL + labelling

GMP_requirements.regulatory_basis: Part C, Division 2 (C.02.001-C.02.030) of Food and Drug Regulations

establishment_licensing.description: All manufacturers, packagers, labellers, importers, distributors, and testers of drugs in Canada must hold an Establishment Licence (EL)
establishment_licensing.annual_review: Annual Licence Review required per C.01A.009
establishment_licensing.fda_equivalent: FDA Facility Registration + Drug Listing (no licence per se; relies on inspection)
establishment_licensing.key_difference: Canada requires a formal licence (EL) with annual review — more prescriptive than FDA registration

GMP_divisions.C.02.003: Sale — drugs must be manufactured in accordance with GMP
GMP_divisions.C.02.006: Personnel — qualified, trained (no explicit QP requirement like EU)
GMP_divisions.C.02.011: Manufacturing Control — written procedures, in-process controls
GMP_divisions.C.02.029: Sterile Products — specific requirements for aseptic processing
GMP_divisions.C.02.030: Medical Gases — specific GMP for medical gases

key_differences_from_fda_cgmp: Canada requires formal Establishment Licence (EL); FDA uses registration + periodic inspection
Canadian GMP is codified in regulations (C.02.xxx); FDA cGMP is in 21 CFR 210/211
No Qualified Person (QP) requirement in Canada (similar to FDA, unlike EU)
Stability program is explicitly required by regulation (C.02.027); FDA guidance-based
Annual Licence Review is a regulatory requirement; FDA has no annual licence review

PICS_membership.description: Canada is a PIC/S member — GMP guide (GUI-0001) incorporates PIC/S annexes directly
PICS_membership.alignment: Substantially harmonized with EU GMP via PIC/S membership, but retains Canadian regulatory numbering (C.02.xxx) and enforcement model

annual_product_reviews.description: NOT explicitly mandated as standalone requirement in Division 2 (unlike FDA 21 CFR 211.180(e) annual product review). GUI-0001 Section 4 recommends product quality reviews consistent with ICH Q10.

key_differences_from_eu_gmp: No Qualified Person (QP) requirement in Canada; EU requires QP for batch release
Canada uses Establishment Licence; EU uses Manufacturing Authorisation
Canadian GMP guide (GUI-0001) is the interpretive guide; EU uses EudraLex Vol 4
Canada adopts PIC/S annexes directly; EU has its own Annex system (substantially similar content via PIC/S harmonization)
Canada has specific medical gases GMP (C.02.030); EU has separate Annex 6
Annual product reviews not mandated in Canadian regulations (recommended in guidance); EU GMP Chapter 1 requires them

Schedule_D_biologics.lot_release: Required for Schedule D drugs (biologics) — manufacturer must submit samples and protocols for lot release per C.04.007
Schedule_D_biologics.reference_preparations: Health Canada maintains reference preparations for biologics per C.04.006
Schedule_D_biologics.fda_equivalent: CBER lot release for specific products (not all biologics)
Schedule_D_biologics.ema_equivalent: Official Batch Release (OCABR) through OMCL network
Schedule_D_biologics.key_difference: Canada's lot release applies broadly to Schedule D drugs; FDA lot release is narrower

ADR_reporting_manufacturers.regulatory_basis: C.01.017 of Food and Drug Regulations
ADR_reporting_manufacturers.annual_summary_reports: Required per C.01.018 — Annual Summary Report and Case Reports
ADR_reporting_manufacturers.issue_related_summary_reports: C.01.019 — may be required by Minister

drug_shortage_reporting.regulatory_basis: C.01.014.7 — Shortages of Drugs and Interruption and Discontinuation of Sale
drug_shortage_reporting.requirement: Manufacturers must report drug shortages and planned discontinuations

summary_basis_of_decision.description: Health Canada publishes a Summary Basis of Decision (SBD) document after issuing a NOC for certain drug submissions
content: Summary of clinical, non-clinical, and quality evaluations
summary_basis_of_decision.fda_equivalent: FDA approval package (medical review, chemistry review, etc.) — more detailed
summary_basis_of_decision.key_difference: Canada's SBD is a single narrative summary; FDA publishes multiple detailed review documents; EMA EPAR is more detailed with multiple modules

NOC_c_confirmatory_studies.description: Post-market confirmatory studies required under NOC/c (Notice of Compliance with Conditions)
NOC_c_confirmatory_studies.consequence_of_failure: NOC/c can be suspended or market authorization withdrawn if confirmatory studies fail

environmental_assessment.description: Environmental Assessment Statement required in Module 1.5 of drug submissions
environmental_assessment.regulatory_basis: Canadian Environmental Protection Act and CEPA notification requirements
environmental_assessment.fda_equivalent: Environmental Assessment (EA) or categorical exclusion under 21 CFR 25 — FDA largely grants exclusions
environmental_assessment.ema_equivalent: Environmental Risk Assessment (ERA) per CHMP/SWP guideline — required for all MAAs
environmental_assessment.key_difference: Canada requires an Environmental Assessment Statement; implementation has been more prescriptive than FDA but less detailed than EMA ERA for certain product types

innovative_drug_data_protection.description: Data protection for innovative drugs under C.08.004.1, implementing CUSMA Articles 20.48-20.49
innovative_drug_data_protection.filing_block_description: No manufacturer may file NDS, ANDS, or supplement based on direct/indirect comparison to the innovative drug before 6 years from first NOC date
innovative_drug_data_protection.NOC_block_description: Minister shall not approve or issue NOC for such submissions until 8 years after innovative drug's first NOC
innovative_drug_data_protection.pediatric_extension_description: Extended to 8.5 years if innovator provides pediatric clinical trial data within 5 years of first NOC and Minister determines health benefit to pediatric populations
innovative_drug_data_protection.innovative_drug_definition: A drug containing a medicinal ingredient not previously approved by Minister (excluding salt, ester, enantiomer, solvate, or polymorph variations)
innovative_drug_data_protection.register: Minister must maintain a register of innovative drugs with data protection information (C.08.004.1(9))
exceptions: Innovative drug not marketed in Canada (C.08.004.1(5))
innovative_drug_data_protection.fda_equivalent: 5 years NCE exclusivity (3 years for new clinical investigations) + 6 months pediatric
innovative_drug_data_protection.ema_equivalent: 8+2+1 years (8 data + 2 market + 1 new indication + 6 months pediatric)
innovative_drug_data_protection.key_difference: Canada uses a 6-year filing block + 8-year NOC block model (CUSMA-aligned); FDA provides only 5 years NCE; EMA provides 8+2 = 10 years effective protection. Canada is between FDA and EMA.

Priority_Review.description: Expedited review for drugs treating serious, life-threatening or severely debilitating diseases with substantial evidence of clinical effectiveness
Priority_Review.review_timeline_reduction: Target 180 days vs. standard 300 days for NDS

NOC_c.description: Notice of Compliance with Conditions — conditional approval with post-market study obligations

SAP.description: Access to non-marketed drugs for serious or life-threatening conditions on case-by-case basis
SAP.regulatory_basis: Part C, Division 10 (C.10.001) — Access to Drugs in Exceptional Circumstances
SAP.fda_equivalent: Expanded Access / Compassionate Use (21 CFR 312 Subpart I)

Extraordinary_Use_NDS.description: Unique Canadian pathway for CBRN (chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear) emergency drugs where human exposure would be lethal/disabling and occurrences are sporadic

New_Drug_Committee_Appeal.description: Unique Canadian appeal mechanism — manufacturer dissatisfied with NOC refusal or suspension can request referral to a 3-member New Drug Committee
New_Drug_Committee_Appeal.composition: 1 member appointed by Minister, 1 by manufacturer, 1 jointly (or by Minister as chair). No government or manufacturer full-time employees.

Emergency_Drug_Authorization.description: Minister may issue letter of authorization for emergency sale of new drug to a practitioner

Public_Health_Emergency_Pre_positioning.description: Allows EL holders to import and distribute public health emergency drugs BEFORE NOC issuance, with Chief Public Health Officer involvement

SGBA_Plus.description: Required in CTAs — Health Canada mandates sex- and gender-based analysis in clinical trial applications
SGBA_Plus.fda_equivalent: Not mandated in IND (FDA has guidance on inclusion but no SGBA equivalent)

TB_screening_Phase_I.description: TB screening required for healthy volunteers in Phase I trials with immunosuppressant drugs

brand_name_confusion_assessment.description: NDS for human-use drugs must include assessment of likelihood of brand name being mistaken for another drug (look-alike/sound-alike assessment)
brand_name_confusion_assessment.fda_equivalent: Not required in NDA (FDA reviews names but does not require applicant assessment in submission)

mandatory_hospital_ADR_reporting.description: Mandatory reporting of serious ADRs and medical device incidents by hospitals
mandatory_hospital_ADR_reporting.fda_equivalent: Voluntary MedWatch reporting (except for specific mandatory reporting)

certificate_of_supplementary_protection.description: Canada's patent term restoration mechanism — extends patent protection for innovative drugs

grounds: (a) Drug not safe for specified use (new clinical evidence or testing methods)
(b) Lack of substantial evidence of effectiveness (new information)
(c) Submission contained untrue statement of material fact
(f) Labelling is false, misleading, or incomplete
(g) For extraordinary use drugs: failure to follow monitoring plan

post_NOC_record_keeping.description: Manufacturers must maintain auditable records for at least 7 years covering animal/clinical experience, scientific literature, testing, labelling errors, lot failures, efficacy failures

clinical_information_disclosure.description: Clinical trial information ceases to be confidential business information when NOC is issued. Minister may disclose without consent.

foreign_action_reporting.description: Mandatory reporting to Health Canada of foreign regulatory actions (suspensions, withdrawals, safety actions taken by other regulators)

CTD_format_requirements.description: Health Canada adopted the ICH CTD format with region-specific Module 1

subsections.1.2.8: Other Application Information (CRP confirmation, waiver requests, certificates of analyses)

subsections.1.4.2: Comprehensive Summary: Bioequivalence (CS-BE) — UNIQUE TO CANADA

CS_BE.description: Canada-unique integrated summary for ANDS submissions that rely on comparative bioavailability studies
content: Physicochemical Characteristics (pKa, molecular weight, solubility, chirality, polymorphism)
Pharmacokinetics (ADME, AUC, Tmax, Cmax, food effects)
Drug Product Classification (4 categories by release type and PK variability)

drug_product_classification.i: Conventional (immediate) release with uncomplicated or non-variable PK
drug_product_classification.ii: Modified release with uncomplicated or non-variable PK
drug_product_classification.iii: Conventional release with complicated or variable PK
drug_product_classification.iv: Modified release with complicated or variable PK

CS_BE.special_rule: If CS-BE is completed for submissions relying SOLELY on pivotal comparative bioavailability studies, Modules 2.4-2.7 of the CTD do NOT need to be completed
CS_BE.fda_equivalent: No direct equivalent — FDA bioequivalence data submitted in ANDA Sections
CS_BE.ema_equivalent: No direct equivalent — EMA generic applications include bioequivalence study reports but no structured CS-BE

electronic_submission.guidance: Preparation of Drug Regulatory Activities in the Non-eCTD Electronic-Only Format

bioequivalence_requirements.regulatory_basis: TPD Guidance: Conduct and Analysis of Bioavailability and Bioequivalence Studies (Parts A and B) + Expert Advisory Committee Report C
bioequivalence_requirements.definition: A high degree of similarity in the bioavailabilities of two pharmaceutical products (of the same galenic form) from the same molar dose, that are unlikely to produce clinically relevant differences in therapeutic effects, or adverse reactions, or both

acceptance_criteria.standard: 90% CI of geometric mean ratio for AUC and Cmax within 80-125%

study_requirements.design: Typically 2-period, 2-sequence, single-dose crossover in healthy volunteers
study_requirements.fasting_and_fed: Fasting study required; fed study may also be required depending on labelling
study_requirements.multiple_dose: May be required for modified-release formulations or drugs with complicated PK

data_submission_format.description: Computer-readable datasets required in specific ASCII format
data_submission_format.appendix: Appendix B of CTD Bioavailability Guidance specifies format

waiver_policies.proportional_formulations: TPD policy on Bioequivalence of Proportional Formulations for solid oral dosage forms
waiver_policies.oral_solutions: Waiver of Comparative Bioavailability Requirements for Oral Solutions
waiver_policies.justification_required: Scientific justification must be provided in Module 1.2.8 for any waiver

labelling_requirements.bilingual: All labelling must be in both English and French per Official Languages Act
labelling_requirements.DIN_on_label: Drug Identification Number must appear on all labels per C.01.014
labelling_requirements.inner_outer_labels: Both inner and outer labels required in submission
labelling_requirements.caution_statements: C.01.028 — Cautionary Statements and Child Resistant Packages
labelling_requirements.prescription_drug_labelling: Specific requirements per C.04.010 for prescription drugs

clinical_trial_labelling.requirements: C.05.011 — specific labelling for clinical trial drugs
clinical_trial_labelling.bilingual: Must be in English and French
clinical_trial_labelling.content: Protocol number, drug identification, dosage instructions, lot number, expiry, storage conditions, sponsor name
